title: Yahaya Bello Not Involved In Property Transactions Before Court – EFCC Witness
description: Yahaya Bello Not Involved In Property Transactions Before Court – EFCC Witness  LEADERSHIP Newspapers
content: The 17th prosecution witness in the ongoing alleged money laundering case filed against the immediate past Governor of Kogi State, Yahaya Bello, and two others, yesterday said that the former governor was not involved in any of the property transactions before the court. The witness, Shehu Bello Bala, told the court, while being cross-examined by the Defendant’s Counsel, Paul Daudu, SAN, that the 2nd defendant did not also participate in any of the transactions. He noted that one Farouk Bello served as the agent. On cross-examination, he, however, noted that he was aware that the title documents – deed of assignment and power of attorney were returned to Efab Estate by Farouk Bello. The witness had earlier said he was not in anyway related to Ali Bello and Farouk Bello, whom he had mentioned in his examination-in-chief. He also confirmed that he was not from Kogi State, implying that he was not also related to the former governor. The witness, a commodity trader, who said he was from Katsina State, noted that he met Ali Bello about 14 years ago. “So you have known him long enough to know the kind of businesses he is into,” the Defendant’s Counsel said. “I knew Ali Bello in the course of my business which is commodity trading. We have done quite a number of businesses like buying grains from the North . “We buy ginger and other products from the North and sell it. This was around 2012-2013. I also know that he is into real estate,” the witness responded. The Counsel to the 2nd and 3rd defendants, A.M. Aliyu, SAN, asked the witness if he knew the 3rd defendant, he said no, noting that he did not also partake in the transactions. He told the court that at the time the title documents were returned to Efab Estate, Farouk Bello, who was his father’s friend, had passed on. “Would I be correct to say that it was the children who said they wanted to collect the money on the property after the death of Dr Farouk Bello?” the Defendant’s Counsel asked. “Someone came to me with documents concerning the property that they wanted to sell the property but I cannot confirm if he was Farouk Bello’s son,” the witness responded. He confirmed that he took the documents back to the chairman of Efab Estate. “That was when we realised that the property was being investigated,” he said. He said the second property that was not marked by the EFCC was the one bought by Farouk. “Will I be correct to say that Ali Bello inherited that property from his mother?” The Defendant’s Counsel asked. The prosecution counsel, Kemi Pinheiro, SAN, interjected that the evidence the witness gave was not about inheritance. RELATED NEWS Gunmen Ambush, Kill District Head In Plateau Community Why Tinubu Deserves Re-election – Ojo Delta Varsity Student Arrested With Locally-made Pistol But the Defendant’s Counsel said he was entitled to lay foundation for his defence and that he could ask the witness within his capacity. We’ve got the edge. Get real-time reports, breaking scoops, and exclusive
summary: The EFCC's testimony may alleviate concerns about property transactions involving high-profile individuals, potentially boosting investor confidence in Lagos' real estate market.

mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: This may positively impact our efforts to shift new-sales mix toward higher-margin segments, such as premium Lakowe Lakes products.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: The increased investor confidence may create opportunities for institutional/bulk transactions and corporate mortgage programmes.

title: Lagos expands oversight of real estate sector
description: Lagos expands oversight of real estate sector  Punch Newspapers
content: Lagos expands oversight of real estate sector June 18, 2026 12:55 am By Ayoola Babalola Kindly share this story: The Lagos State Government has intensified efforts to strengthen oversight of the real estate sector with an inspection of satellite offices established by the Lagos State Real Estate Regulatory Authority across key parts of the state. The Permanent Secretary, Ministry of Housing, Abdulhafis Toriola, recently led a team of management staff on an assessment tour of LASRERA’s satellite offices located in Igbogbo-Baiyeku, Ikorodu, Surulere, Agege and Ojodu Local Government and Local Council Development Areas. The inspection focused on evaluating the operational readiness, facilities and service delivery capacity of the centres, which were established to bring regulatory services closer to residents, property owners, developers and other stakeholders in the real estate sector. Officials of the host local government councils received the delegation and praised the state’s efforts to decentralise regulatory services, describing the initiative as a step towards improving accessibility, efficiency and public engagement. Speaking during the visit, Toriola reaffirmed the government’s commitment to strengthening LASRERA’s mandate of sanitising the real estate sector, curbing fraudulent practices and promoting compliance with established regulations. He said the authority remained a critical component of the state’s efforts to protect residents and restore confidence in property transactions. Re: FCCPC and the Presidency’s FDI drive LASG intensifies HIV awareness campaign for PWDs Lagos partners Foundation to boost road safety awareness According to the permanent secretary, the government was preparing to further expand the agency’s presence across Lagos, with new satellite offices expected to commence operations in Amuwo-Odofin, Mushin, Ojo and Lekki Local Government and Local Council Development Areas. The planned expansion, he said, was designed to deepen public access to regulatory services and ensure quicker response to real estate-related complaints and compliance issues. Toriola also commended local government authorities for their support and cooperation, noting that effective collaboration between state agencies and grassroots institutions is essential to achieving a transparent, accountable and well-regulated property market. The inspection visit forms part of broader efforts by the Lagos State Government to strengthen institutional capacity and improve service delivery within the real estate sector, amid growing demand for regulatory oversight in one of Nigeria’s largest property markets. With additional satellite offices set to open in strategic locations across the state, officials say residents will have easier access to LASRERA’s services, helping to improve compliance, enhance consumer protection and tackle fraudulent activities within the industry. Ayoola Babalola Kindly share this story: All rights reserved. This material, and ot
summary: The Lagos State Government's intensified oversight of the real estate sector through LASRERA's satellite offices may lead to increased regulatory scrutiny, potentially benefiting Mixta Africa's premium segments and institutional transactions, while posing a risk to affordable housing projects.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This may benefit our premium segments, such as Lakowe Lakes products, and institutional transactions, like the MREIF/MOFI diaspora and domestic mortgage partnership with Access Bank.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Increased regulatory scrutiny may lead to higher costs for developers, potentially affecting our pricing and costs.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: The state's efforts may create opportunities for Mixta Africa to demonstrate its compliance and credibility, enhancing our DFI credibility.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The risk of increased regulatory scrutiny may threaten the execution of our affordable housing projects, such as Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe.

title: The Impact of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in the Nigerian Real Estate Landscape
description: The Impact of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in the Nigerian Real Estate Landscape  THISDAYLIVE
content: Wednesday, 17th June, 2026 Home Business Politics Nigeria International Africa Rest of the World Health & Wellbeing Education Life & Style Sport Editorial Backpage Breaking News Appeal Court Suspends Lifu’s Order to Deregister Parties 9 hours ago Tinubu Hails Troops for Killing Bandit Kingpin, Bastuji, Declares It’s Notable Blow 9 hours ago In New Regulatory Masterstroke, CBN Orders Banks, Fintechs to Disclose Beneficial Owners, Localise Payments Data by 2027 9 hours ago IMF: Nigeria Risks Rise in Illicit Finance, Money Laundering Amid $59bn Crypto Inflow 9 hours ago Latest Headlines Rising Cyber Threats Could Undermine Business Sustainability, Profitability, ISSAN Warns IsDB 2026: Youth Devt Forum Highlights Regional Integration as a Pathway to Youth Empowerment, Shared Prosperity Great Nigerian Insurance’s N15.6bn Rights Issue Opens Stephanie Oforka: Why Stewardship is the New Standard for Nigerian Executives The Impact of Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in the Nigerian Real Estate Landscape Featured | 14 hours ago Shares Share Post Email Share Share ESV Usman, Murtala Yusuf, Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) has become one of the most important drivers of growth in Nigeria’s real estate sector. As Africa’s most populous nation continues to urbanize rapidly, the demand for residential, commercial, industrial, and mixed-use developments has increased significantly. In this context, foreign investment has played a crucial role in shaping the country’s real estate landscape, bringing not only capital but also expertise, technology, and global best practices. One of the most visible impacts of FDI is the injection of much-needed capital into large-scale real estate projects. Nigeria faces a housing deficit estimated in the millions of units, alongside growing demand for office spaces, shopping malls, industrial parks, and hospitality facilities. Local financing alone has often been insufficient to meet these needs due to high borrowing costs and limited access to long-term funds. Foreign investors help bridge this gap by providing the financial resources necessary to develop major projects that might otherwise remain unrealized. Beyond capital, FDI has contributed significantly to the modernization of Nigeria’s real estate sector. Foreign investors often introduce advanced construction techniques, innovative project management systems, and international standards in property development. These contributions have improved the quality of buildings and enhanced the competitiveness of the Nigerian property market. The emergence of smart buildings, integrated estates, and modern retail complexes reflects the influence of foreign expertise on the sector. Furthermore, foreign investment has stimulated economic growth through job creation and the development of supporting industries. Real estate projects generate employment opportunities for architects, engineers, contractors, artisans, suppliers, and facility managers. As more developments are undertaken, t
summary: The article highlights a surge in foreign direct investment (FDI) into Nigeria’s real estate sector, underscoring stronger capital flows, heightened institutional interest, and a shift toward premium and commercial projects, especially in Lagos. This influx is expected to accelerate demand for high‑margin assets and bolster financing options, reinforcing Lagos’s position as the primary hub for real‑estate growth.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: FDI aligns with Mixta’s priority to shift new‑sales mix toward higher‑margin premium Lakowe Lakes products and to expand bulk institutional transactions, potentially accelerating sales of Lakowe Crossings and Lakowe Annexe.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Increased foreign capital may lift land and construction costs in Lagos, tighten competition for premium sites, and prompt tighter regulatory scrutiny on foreign ownership structures.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Leverage the growing diaspora buyer channel (O4S framework) and institutional mortgage programmes (MREIF/MOFI, Access Bank) to capture bulk sales and secure financing at favourable terms.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Potential currency volatility and evolving FDI‑related regulations could affect pricing models and delay project financing if not proactively managed.

title: How Lack of Funds is Stalling Real Estate Development in Nigeria
description: How Lack of Funds is Stalling Real Estate Development in Nigeria  THISDAYLIVE
content: Wednesday, 17th June, 2026 Home Business Politics Nigeria International Africa Rest of the World Health & Wellbeing Education Life & Style Sport Editorial Backpage Breaking News Appeal Court Suspends Lifu’s Order to Deregister Parties 9 hours ago Tinubu Hails Troops for Killing Bandit Kingpin, Bastuji, Declares It’s Notable Blow 9 hours ago In New Regulatory Masterstroke, CBN Orders Banks, Fintechs to Disclose Beneficial Owners, Localise Payments Data by 2027 9 hours ago IMF: Nigeria Risks Rise in Illicit Finance, Money Laundering Amid $59bn Crypto Inflow 9 hours ago Latest Headlines Rising Cyber Threats Could Undermine Business Sustainability, Profitability, ISSAN Warns IsDB 2026: Youth Devt Forum Highlights Regional Integration as a Pathway to Youth Empowerment, Shared Prosperity Great Nigerian Insurance’s N15.6bn Rights Issue Opens Stephanie Oforka: Why Stewardship is the New Standard for Nigerian Executives How Lack of Funds is Stalling Real Estate Development in Nigeria Featured | 14 hours ago Shares Share Post Email Share Share ESV Usman, Murtala Yusuf, Nigeria’s real estate sector has long been recognized as a critical driver of economic growth, job creation, and urban development. With a rapidly growing population, increasing urbanization, and a housing deficit estimated in the millions of units, the demand for residential and commercial properties continues to rise. However, despite the enormous opportunities within the sector, one major challenge continues to hinder its growth: lack of adequate funding. Access to finance remains one of the biggest obstacles facing real estate developers in Nigeria. Developing housing estates, office complexes, shopping centers, and other property projects requires substantial capital. Unfortunately, many developers struggle to secure the funding needed to execute these projects. Commercial bank loans, which are often the most accessible source of financing, come with high interest rates and short repayment periods that are unsuitable for long-term real estate investments. The high cost of borrowing has forced many developers to either abandon projects or significantly slow down construction activities. In some cases, partially completed buildings remain abandoned for years because developers can no longer sustain the financial burden. This not only affects investors but also contributes to the growing housing shortage across the country. Another challenge is the limited availability of mortgage financing. In developed economies, mortgages play a key role in supporting both property developers and homebuyers. In Nigeria, however, access to affordable mortgage loans remains difficult for many citizens due to stringent requirements, high interest rates, and low income levels. As a result, developers often struggle to find enough buyers who can afford newly built homes, reducing the incentive to invest in large-scale housing projects. The impact of inadequate funding extends beyond developers. It also affec
summary: The lack of funds in Nigeria is stalling real estate development, posing a significant threat to Lagos' real estate market, particularly in the affordable and premium segments, and may impact Mixta Africa's active projects and strategic priorities.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This may impact our ability to recover concentrated receivables and pursue institutional/bulk transactions, as well as our plans to scale Lagos New Town (LNT) and find a healthcare operator partner for the proposed Lakowe Wellness and Medical Center.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The lack of funds may lead to increased costs and reduced pricing power, affecting our pricing strategy and competitiveness in the market.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: This may create an opportunity for Mixta Africa to explore alternative funding sources and partnerships, such as the diaspora buyer channel via the 'Own It 4 Sure' (O4S) S.U.R.E. framework.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: This poses a significant risk to Mixta Africa's execution of its strategic priorities, particularly in the near-term.

title: Jim Ovia, Akwa Ibom and Lagos Property Market
description: Jim Ovia, Akwa Ibom and Lagos Property Market  THISDAYLIVE
content: Wednesday, 17th June, 2026 Home Business Politics Nigeria International Africa Rest of the World Health & Wellbeing Education Life & Style Sport Editorial Backpage Breaking News Appeal Court Suspends Lifu’s Order to Deregister Parties 11 hours ago Tinubu Hails Troops for Killing Bandit Kingpin, Bastuji, Declares It’s Notable Blow 11 hours ago In New Regulatory Masterstroke, CBN Orders Banks, Fintechs to Disclose Beneficial Owners, Localise Payments Data by 2027 11 hours ago IMF: Nigeria Risks Rise in Illicit Finance, Money Laundering Amid $59bn Crypto Inflow 11 hours ago Latest Headlines Jim Ovia, Akwa Ibom and Lagos Property Market International Day of the African Child: FG Assures Safe Return Abducted Schoolchildren Govt Pushing Nigeria to the Brink, Prioritising 2027 Over Citizens’ Welfare, Says Reps Minority Caucus Obidient Movement Dismisses Rift Reports, Insists It Remains United Jim Ovia, Akwa Ibom and Lagos Property Market Nigeria | 20 minutes ago Shares Share Post Email Share Share Etim Etim Founder of Zenith Bank, Jim Ovia, is expanding his business portfolio into the luxury end of Nigeria’s real estate business, betting that high-end property offers stronger returns than banking. Not many would be convinced by that, but coming from a man who founded the bank 35 years ago and grew it into one of the biggest lenders in the country, the statement captured attention across sectors. Ovia announced his retirement from banking as Chairman of Zenith last month. His company, Quantum Luxury Properties, is developing two major high-end residential towers in Lagos: the first, Metropolitan Towers, is a 26-floor high-rise where each apartment would sell for $1.85 million; and the second, Quantum Luxury Towers, with 44 apartments, each starting from $2.8 million selling price. Two decades ago, Ovia had entered the property business with the development of high-end event centres in Victoria Island. The success of The Civic Centre on the highbrow Ozumba Mbadiwe Road in Victoria Island certainly prepared the 74-year-old for a life in retirement. Lagos luxury property market is one of the biggest and most profitable in Africa. According to intelligence firm, Estate Intel, about 135 homes in Lagos are currently priced above $1 million each. The market is projected to grow substantially over the next five years, with nearly 1,400 additional luxury homes projected to enter the market by 2029. Akwa Ibom State government is adding 32 new apartments to the expected 1,400. Located in Victoria Island and developed by the state government, Ibom Towers is an 18-storey investment that will feature 10 one-bedroom units; 10 two-bedroom; nine three-bedroom units; and three two-bedroom penthouses and services quarters. I attended the turning of the sod event at the construction site in October 2024. Construction has been progressing steadily since. Ibom Towers is designed as an income-generating venture for the state whose IGR has been among the least in the country. C
summary: Jim Ovia’s remarks underscore robust demand for premium residential and commercial assets in Lagos, highlighting growing diaspora investment and institutional interest, while noting Akwa Ibom’s emerging role as a secondary growth hub.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: Positive sentiment reinforces Mixta’s shift toward premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots, supporting revenue targets and sales mix objectives.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Elevated investor confidence may lift price expectations and accelerate financing terms, affecting cost structures and competitive positioning.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Leverage Ovia’s network to deepen institutional/bulk transaction pipelines and expand the diaspora channel via O4S framework.

title: Minister: FG’s Ibeju-Lekki Housing Project 90% Complete
description: Minister: FG’s Ibeju-Lekki Housing Project 90% Complete  THISDAYLIVE
content: Wednesday, 17th June, 2026 Home Business Politics Nigeria International Africa Rest of the World Health & Wellbeing Education Life & Style Sport Editorial Backpage Breaking News Appeal Court Suspends Lifu’s Order to Deregister Parties 9 hours ago Tinubu Hails Troops for Killing Bandit Kingpin, Bastuji, Declares It’s Notable Blow 9 hours ago In New Regulatory Masterstroke, CBN Orders Banks, Fintechs to Disclose Beneficial Owners, Localise Payments Data by 2027 9 hours ago IMF: Nigeria Risks Rise in Illicit Finance, Money Laundering Amid $59bn Crypto Inflow 9 hours ago Latest Headlines Rising Cyber Threats Could Undermine Business Sustainability, Profitability, ISSAN Warns IsDB 2026: Youth Devt Forum Highlights Regional Integration as a Pathway to Youth Empowerment, Shared Prosperity Great Nigerian Insurance’s N15.6bn Rights Issue Opens Stephanie Oforka: Why Stewardship is the New Standard for Nigerian Executives Minister: FG’s Ibeju-Lekki Housing Project 90% Complete Business | 2 days ago Shares Share Post Email Share Share As part of his ongoing nationwide assessment of housing projects, the Minister of Housing and Urban Development, Muttaqha Darma, has inspected the Lagos ‘Renewed Hope City’ Project located in Ibeju-Lekki, Lagos State, revealing a 90 per cent completion rate. The inspection visit was aimed at assessing the progress of the project, which comprises a total of 2,084 housing units, ahead of its planned commissioning in October, a statement from the ministry said. Darma said the project was being delivered under a Public-Private Partnership (PPP) arrangement between the federal government and private sector developers, saying that the model remains a strategic approach to accelerating housing delivery across the country. He noted that the FMBN financing would provide shelter for thousands of Nigerians, describing the intervention as a demonstration of the bank’s commitment to delivering on its mandate. Expressing satisfaction with the level of work completed, Darma stated that the project was nearing readiness for offtake. “I have seen more than 80 per cent completion. In fact, I can say between 85 and 90 per cent because all of the houses already have fittings. Some of them are ready for occupation,” he said. Speaking during the inspection, the Managing Director/Chief Executive of FMBN, Shehu Osidi, reaffirmed the bank’s commitment to delivering affordable housing solutions and ensuring that completed homes reach their intended beneficiaries. Osidi urged developers to adopt faster delivery timelines to enable beneficiaries to take possession of completed housing units without unnecessary delays. title: Short-let apartments take over property market as annual rent skyrockets
description: Short-let apartments take over property market as annual rent skyrockets  Daily Trust
content: As the cost of annual property rent continue to rise in major states across the country, short-term rental market which is driven by platforms like Airbnb, is gradually becoming the new alternative for Nigerians With housing shortages becoming more pronounced in major cities, particularly due to rapid urbanisation and a rising population, many Nigerians are turning to short-let properties for flexibility and affordability. In cities such as Lagos, Port Harcourt, and Abuja, short-term rentals and serviced apartments are becoming a preferred investment strategy. Checks by Daily Trust has shown that annual property rent has increased by over 300 per cent in the last three years This shift is fuelled by the high costs of long-term rentals, which are out of reach for many middle-class families, and the increasing influx of tourists, expatriates, and business professionals who seek temporary accommodations. The growing popularity of platforms like Airbnb and local short-let services has further fuelled this market expansion, making it an essential part of Nigeria’s housing landscape. In return, property owners are capitalising on the lucrative opportunities that short-term rentals provide, offering fully furnished homes to meet the demand for quality but temporary housing solutions. A recent report shows that the short-let market in Lagos State, Nigeria’s commercial capital, is gaining momentum, with revenue projected to rise to N300 bn in 2025 from N264.3 bn last year, a report by Edala Homes stated. The report, titled ‘Lagos Short-let Market 2024’, surveyed 5,806 listings, with 78 per cent of the data sourced from AirDNA and the remaining 22 per cent tracked by Edala Homes It was developed to provide in-depth insight into the Lagos short-let market, focusing on the performance of various submarkets. The report said, “In 2024 alone, the Lagos short-let market’s total estimated revenue was N264.3 bn. This figure underscores the sector’s significance in the broader hospitality industry and its contribution to Lagos’s economy. “Key demand drivers include Lagos’ strategic positioning as a business and leisure hub, an influx of domestic and international events, and the rising popularity of remote work and digital nomadism, and the forecast for 2025 indicates a total market revenue of N300 bn, marking a significant growth trajectory. “The market experienced substantial growth in 2024, driven by increasing demand for flexible and luxurious accommodations across various submarkets. As a vital component of Nigeria’s hospitality sector, Lagos’s short-let market caters to business travellers, tourists, expatriates, and local professionals.” A breakdown of the report revealed that Ikoyi led in luxury accommodations, generating N37.5 bn, and revenue is forecasted to reach N42 bn. Victoria Island, balancing luxury and accessibility, recorded N19.3 bn, with expected growth to N21.6 bn, and Banana Island, an exclusive market, contributed N11 bn, expected to reach N1
summary: The Lagos real estate market is witnessing a significant shift towards short-term rentals, driven by rising property rent costs and housing shortages, which poses a critical threat to the affordability of long-term rentals and may create opportunities for Mixta Africa's premium products and serviced apartments.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This trend may create opportunities for Mixta Africa's premium products and serviced apartments, such as Lakowe Lakes, which can cater to the growing demand for short-term rentals.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The increasing popularity of short-term rentals may lead to a shift in pricing and costs, potentially affecting Mixta Africa's revenue targets and profitability.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta Africa can capitalize on this trend by developing more serviced apartments and short-term rental properties, particularly in high-demand areas like Lekki and Ibeju-Lekki.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The rising costs of property rent and housing shortages may threaten Mixta Africa's ability to recover concentrated receivables and meet its revenue targets.

title: 1,859 families access N128b mortgages as Tinubu pushes affordable housing drive
description: 1,859 families access N128b mortgages as Tinubu pushes affordable housing drive  The Guardian Nigeria News
content: 1,859 families access N128b mortgages as Tinubu pushes affordable housing drive  The Guardian Nigeria News
summary: The federal government's rollout of N128 billion in mortgages for 1,859 families signals a strong policy push for affordable housing in Lagos, expanding credit access and likely accelerating demand for low‑to‑mid‑range units. This development will intensify competition in the affordable segment while creating new financing pathways for developers aligned with government initiatives.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: The expanded mortgage pool directly supports sales of Mixta's affordable‑tier projects (Lakowe Crossings, Lakowe Annexe) and can accelerate uptake of lower‑cost phases within Lagos New Town.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Increased financing availability may tighten pricing competition in the affordable segment and raise regulatory scrutiny on mortgage eligibility and documentation standards.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta can leverage its institutional mortgage partnerships (MREIF/MOFI, Access Bank) and diaspora sales channel to capture bulk transactions and diversify funding sources for affordable units.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: A shift in buyer preference toward government‑subsidised affordable units could dilute the intended premium‑mix sales strategy and increase exposure to policy‑driven demand fluctuations.

title: Real Estate - Nigeria
content: Market Insights Finance Real Estate - Nigeria Nigeria Highlights Market definition In-Scope/Out-of-scope Market structure Methodology In Nigeria, the Real Estate market market is anticipated to achieve a value of US$2.42tn by 2026. Among the various segments within the market, Residential Real Estate holds the largest share, with a projected market volume of US$2.05tn by 2026. Over the period of 2026-2031, the market is expected to exhibit a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 3.15%, resulting in a market volume of US$2.83tn by 2031. When compared globally, China is projected to generate the highest value in the Real Estate market sector, amounting to US$133.2tn in 2026. The real estate market in Nigeria is experiencing a surge in demand for luxury apartments in major cities. Key regions: United States, China, Japan, Germany, United Kingdom Definition: The real estate market refers to the transaction of properties, including residential properties (such as houses and apartments) and commercial properties, such as office buildings and industrial properties. Apart from property sales, this market includes leases and the value of real estate. Structure: The real estate market comprises residential and commercial real estate. The residential real estate market covers real estate transactions and leases, and residential real estate leases are divided into apartment and house leases. Additional information: The market contains the following KPIs: real estate value aggregated for all countries and regions, average real estate value, real estate transaction revenue, number of real estate sold, number of leased and owned real estate, average room per resident, and dwelling type shares. The dwelling type shares comprise the share of the population that lives in houses and apartments. These shares are displayed for real estate owners and real estate renters as well. In-Scope Residential real estate (houses and apartments) Commercial real estate (office buildings, retail spaces, warehouses, industrial properties) Out-Of-Scope Real estate agencies Construction companies Accommodation services, such as Airbnb Commercial real estate leases and transactions Publicly owned buildings used by the local government Buildings used for public health care services Real Estate Commercial Real Estate Residential Real Estate Residential Real Estate Leases Residential Real Estate Transactions Related markets: Banking Wealth Management Capital Raising Digital Assets Corporate Finance Commodities Insurances Stocks Private Equity Investment Funds Payments Download PDFFAQs and more info on the methodology Value Value Value Change Notes: Data was converted from local currencies using average exchange rates of the respective year. Most recent update: Apr 2026 Source: Statista Market Insights Real Estate Value Comparison Notes: Data was converted from local currencies using average exchange rates of the respective year. Most recent update: Apr 2026 Source: Statista Market Insigh
summary: The Nigerian real estate market is expected to reach a value of US$2.42tn by 2026, with the residential segment holding the largest share, presenting a significant growth opportunity for Mixta Africa's premium and commercial projects, particularly in Lagos.

mixta_relevance.direct_impact: This growth opportunity aligns with Mixta Africa's strategic priority of shifting new-sales mix toward higher-margin segments, such as premium Lakowe Lakes products and LNT commercial plots.
mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The surge in demand for luxury apartments in major cities may impact pricing and costs for Mixta Africa's projects, while the growth of the real estate market may also create opportunities for institutional and bulk transactions.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: The expected growth of the real estate market presents a strategic opportunity for Mixta Africa to expand its premium and commercial offerings, potentially increasing revenue and market share.
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: The rapid growth of the real estate market may also pose a risk of over-supply, particularly in the premium segment, which could impact Mixta Africa's execution and revenue targets.
